skill.name: routeros-container
skill.description: RouterOS /container subsystem for running OCI containers on MikroTik devices. Use when: enabling containers on RouterOS, setting up VETH/bridge networking for containers, managing container lifecycle via CLI or REST API, building OCI images for RouterOS, configuring container environment variables, troubleshooting container issues, or when the user mentions RouterOS container, /container, VETH, device-mode container, or MikroTik Docker.
skill.summary: Manage OCI containers on MikroTik RouterOS devices with CLI and REST API
skill.author: tikoci

security_audit.summary: This is a legitimate documentation file for RouterOS container management skill. Static analyzer flagged 170 potential issues, but all are false positives: backtick markers are markdown formatting, btoa() is legitimate base64 encoding for HTTP Basic Auth, and fetch calls are TypeScript code examples in documentation. The skill provides helpful guidance for managing OCI containers on MikroTik hardware.

medium_findings.title: Documentation Contains Code Examples with Network Access
medium_findings.description: The SKILL.md file includes TypeScript code examples showing HTTP fetch calls to RouterOS REST API endpoints. These are legitimate documentation examples for API usage, not malicious network calls.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The fetch calls are part of TypeScript code examples in documentation demonstrating legitimate RouterOS REST API usage for container management

low_findings.title: Hardcoded Example IP Addresses
low_findings.description: Documentation contains example IP addresses (192.168.1.1) and URLs for educational purposes. These are standard documentation patterns, not security concerns.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Example IP addresses are standard practice in documentation for network tutorials

low_findings.title: Base64 Encoding for Authentication
low_findings.description: The btoa() function is used to encode credentials for HTTP Basic Authentication in RouterOS REST API examples. This is the documented method for RouterOS API authentication.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: btoa() is the correct method for RouterOS Basic Auth and is used in official MikroTik documentation

content.user_title: Manage MikroTik RouterOS Containers with Claude Code
content.value_statement: Setting up and managing OCI containers on MikroTik routers is complex and requires specialized knowledge of RouterOS networking and the container subsystem. This skill provides comprehensive guidance for deploying containers on RouterOS 7.x with proper VETH, bridge, and NAT configuration.
actual_capabilities: Enable device-mode and install container packages on RouterOS 7.x
Configure VETH interfaces and bridge networking for container connectivity
Set up NAT and firewall rules for container internet access and port forwarding
Manage container lifecycle using both CLI and REST API
Configure environment variables and volume mounts for containers
Import local OCI images or pull from container registries
limitations: Requires RouterOS 7.x with container package - RouterOS 6.x not supported
Device-mode requires physical access for initial activation (cannot be done remotely)
Container images must be single-layer, uncompressed Docker v1 format for local import
MIPS architecture routers do not support containers

use_cases.title: Network Engineer Deploying Pi-hole
use_cases.description: A network engineer wants to run Pi-hole DNS sinkhole as a container on their MikroTik router to block ads network-wide without additional hardware.

use_cases.title: DevOps Engineer Setting Up Monitoring
use_cases.description: A DevOps team needs to deploy a lightweight monitoring agent as a container on edge routers to collect network telemetry from remote locations.

use_cases.title: IT Consultant Configuring L2 Access
use_cases.description: An IT consultant needs to run netinstall functionality in a container with direct Layer 2 access to provision devices on a specific network segment.

prompt_templates.title: Getting Started with Containers
prompt_templates.prompt: How do I enable the container subsystem on my MikroTik router running RouterOS 7.22?
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner user setting up containers for the first time

prompt_templates.title: Networking Configuration
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me set up VETH networking with bridge and NAT so my containers can access the internet and I can forward port 80 to a web server running in a container at 172.17.0.2.

prompt_templates.title: Container Lifecycle Management
prompt_templates.prompt: Write RouterOS CLI commands to create, start, stop, view logs, and remove a container named 'monitoring' using a local image file.
prompt_templates.scenario: User needs complete container lifecycle commands

prompt_templates.title: REST API Automation
prompt_templates.prompt: Show me how to use the RouterOS REST API with TypeScript to list all containers, check their status, and start a specific container by ID.

output_examples.input: How do I enable container mode?
output_examples.output:
To enable container mode on RouterOS 7.x, use the following commands:

1. Enable container mode: /system/device-mode/update mode=advanced container=yes

2. Physically confirm the change within the activation timeout by pressing the reset button OR power cycling the device.

Device-mode is a security feature that gates container functionality and must be confirmed physically for security reasons.

output_examples.input: Show me the networking setup for containers
output_examples.output:
Container networking requires three components:

1. VETH interface: /interface/veth/add name=veth-myapp address=172.17.0.2/24 gateway=172.17.0.1

2. Bridge: /interface/bridge/add name=containers then /interface/bridge/port/add bridge=containers interface=veth-myapp

3. Gateway IP: /ip/address/add address=172.17.0.1/24 interface=containers

4. NAT: /ip/firewall/nat/add chain=srcnat action=masquerade src-address=172.17.0.0/24

output_examples.input: How do I pull and run an Alpine container?
output_examples.output:
Use these steps:

1. Set registry: /container/config/set registry-url=https://registry-1.docker.io tmpdir=disk1/pull

2. Pull image: /container/add remote-image=library/alpine:latest interface=veth-myapp

3. Start container: /container/start [find tag~"alpine"]

4. Check logs: /log/print where topics~"container"

best_practices: Always store container data on external USB storage rather than internal flash to avoid wearing out the device
Use inline env and mount syntax (RouterOS 7.21+) to keep container configuration self-contained
Configure logging=yes to capture container output in RouterOS logs for troubleshooting
anti_patterns: Do not attempt to run containers on RouterOS 6.x or MIPS architecture - containers require RouterOS 7.x on ARM, ARM64, or x86
Do not delete a container while it is still running - always stop first and poll .running until "false"
Do not use multi-layer or gzip-compressed images - RouterOS only supports single-layer uncompressed Docker v1 tar format

faq.question: What RouterOS versions support containers?
faq.answer: Containers require RouterOS 7.x. RouterOS 6.x does not support the container subsystem. Your device must also run ARM, ARM64, or x86 architecture - MIPS is not supported.

faq.question: Why do I need physical access to enable containers?
faq.answer: RouterOS device-mode requires physical confirmation (reset button or power cycle) as a security measure. This prevents remote attackers from enabling privileged features. This is by design and cannot be bypassed remotely.

faq.question: Can I run Docker images directly on RouterOS?
faq.answer: Not directly. RouterOS containers require OCI images in Docker v1 single-layer uncompressed format. Standard multi-layer Docker images must be converted using tools like the OCI image building skill.

faq.question: How do I expose services running in a container to the outside network?
faq.answer: Use NAT firewall rules with dstnat action to forward host ports to container IPs. For example: /ip/firewall/nat/add chain=dstnat dst-port=8080 protocol=tcp to-addresses=172.17.0.2 to-ports=80

faq.question: Why are my container environment variables not working?
faq.answer: Check the RouterOS version - pre-7.20 had no env list grouping, 7.20 uses envlists= (plural), and 7.21+ supports inline env= syntax. Always verify with /console/inspect on your specific version.

faq.question: How do I troubleshoot a container that will not start?
faq.answer: Enable logging=yes when creating the container, then check logs with /log/print where topics~container. Also verify the VETH interface exists, the root-dir path is valid, and the container image format is correct.
